my_thoughts: The logical chain building works: 'And what is shameful is bad, isn't it?' continues the chain. The student agreed (cooperative engagement), which shows they understand: doing injustice = shameful (logical step accepted). They'll likely agree to the next logical step next. Hypothesis: Socratic logical chains maintain compliance when each step follows naturally from student's own commitments—testable by tracking whether logical chains maintain compliance better than isolated questions.
accumulated_insight: Two-turn pattern: T1: Student asserted preference ('To suffer it. No one would choose the other')—defensive rationalization via pushes back. They understand: suffering injustice = preferable (moral stance). The comparison_contrast mechanism asks: agreement check ('So you agree that doing injustice is shameful?'). T2: Student agreed to logical step ('Of course—everyone admits that')—cooperative engagement via short affirmation. They understand: doing injustice = shameful (logical step accepted). The comparison_contrast mechanism builds: logical chain continues ('And what is shameful is bad, isn't it?'), links: shameful → bad. Pattern: moral assertion → agreement check → logical step → [next: final conclusion]. They're grasping: logical chain building (shameful → bad → worse).
visible_response: And what is shameful is bad, isn’t it?
